schools.summary: Judah goes down from his brethren to Adullam: 'and it came to pass at that time, that Judah went down from his brethren, and turned in to a certain Adullamite, whose name was Hirah' — Judah separating himself from his brethren (the family of Jacob) and going down to dwell with an Adullamite (a Canaanite of Adullam, the going 'down,' morally as well as geographically, the departure from the covenant household into Canaanite company, the setting for the sad history of the chapter); this history set 'at that time' (about the time of Joseph's sale, ch. 37, the chapter interrupting the Joseph narrative, the contrast, the fathers note, between Judah's degradation among the Canaanites, ch. 38, and Joseph's purity in Egypt, ch. 39, the dark interlude); 'turned in to a certain Adullamite, whose name was Hirah' (Judah consorting with a Canaanite, Hirah, the mingling with the people of the land, the very thing the patriarchs shunned, 24:3, the descent that led to the Canaanite marriage, v.2, and the sad events following, the warning of Judah's worldly turn) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass at that time, that Judah went down from his brethren, and turned in to a certain Adullamite, whose name was Hiras
Judah going down from his brethren to Adullam
the departure from the covenant household into Canaanite company (24:3)
the dark interlude, set against Joseph's purity (ch. 39)

schools.summary: Judah marries a Canaanite woman: 'and Judah saw there a daughter of a certain Canaanite, whose name was Shuah; and he took her, and went in unto her' — Judah marrying the daughter of Shua, a Canaanite (the daughter herself unnamed, called 'Bath-shua,' daughter of Shua, 1 Chronicles 2:3, the Canaanite marriage, the very thing Abraham and Isaac had forbidden, 24:3; 28:1, the worldly match, the descent continued); the Canaanite marriage — Judah, unlike his fathers (who sought wives of the kindred, chs. 24, 28), marrying a daughter of Canaan (the mingling with the accursed line, the fault that bred the sad history, the wicked sons, vv.7, 10, the warning against unequal, worldly marriage); 'he took her, and went in unto her' (the marriage consummated, the Canaanite wife who bore Er, Onan, and Shelah, vv.3-5, the union that brought forth the wicked sons, the sad fruit of the worldly match) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Judah saw there a daughter of a certain Canaanite, whose name was Shuah; and he took her, and went in unto her
Judah marrying a Canaanite woman
the marriage his fathers had forbidden (24:3; 28:1)
the union that bore the wicked sons (vv.3-5, 7, 10)

schools.summary: The birth of Er: 'and she conceived, and bare a son; and he called his name Er' — Judah's Canaanite wife bearing a first son, Er ('watcher,' or 'awake,' the firstborn of Judah, the son who would prove wicked, v.7, the beginning of the sad family history); the first son, Er — Judah's firstborn by the Canaanite wife (the son who would marry Tamar, v.6, and be slain for his wickedness, v.7); 'he called his name Er' (the firstborn named Er, the son whose wickedness, v.7, would leave Tamar a childless widow, the beginning of the levirate history, vv.8-11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And she conceived, and bare a son; and he called his name Er
the birth of Er
Judah's firstborn by the Canaanite wife
the wicked son who would be slain (v.7), leaving Tamar a widow

schools.summary: The birth of Onan: 'and she conceived again, and bare a son; and she called his name Onan' — Judah's wife bearing a second son, Onan ('strong,' or 'vigorous,' the second son of Judah, the son who would sin in the levirate duty and be slain, vv.8-10, the family history continuing); the second son, Onan — Judah's second by the Canaanite wife (the son who would refuse the levirate duty to Tamar, vv.8-9, and be slain, v.10); 'she called his name Onan' (the second son named Onan, the son whose sin, vv.9-10, gave rise to the term 'onanism,' the levirate refuser, the second wicked son) — Gill.

themes: And she conceived again, and bare a son; and she called his name Onan
the birth of Onan
Judah's second son by the Canaanite wife
the levirate refuser who would be slain (vv.9-10)

schools.summary: The birth of Shelah: 'and she yet again conceived, and bare a son; and called his name Shelah: and he was at Chezib, when she bare him' — Judah's wife bearing a third son, Shelah (the third son of Judah, born at Chezib, a place, perhaps Achzib, the son who would be withheld from Tamar, v.11, 14, 26, the occasion of her stratagem); the third son, Shelah — the one Judah promised to Tamar but withheld (v.11, the son who survived, unlike Er and Onan, the head of a family of Judah, the Shelanites, Numbers 26:20); 'he was at Chezib, when she bare him' (the place of Shelah's birth, Chezib/Achzib, the notice of the place, the third son who would figure in the levirate history, vv.11, 14, 26) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And she yet again conceived, and bare a son; and called his name Shelah: and he was at Chezib, when she bare him
the birth of Shelah
Judah's third son, the one withheld from Tamar (v.11)
the occasion of her stratagem (vv.14, 26)

schools.summary: Judah takes Tamar as wife for Er: 'and Judah took a wife for Er his firstborn, whose name was Tamar' — Judah, as father, choosing a wife for his firstborn Er: Tamar ('palm tree,' the marriage arranged by the father, the custom, Tamar, probably a Canaanite, or by some an Aramean, given to Er, the wife who would be widowed by Er's death, v.7, and become the central figure of the chapter); Tamar married to Er, Judah's firstborn (v.3) — the wife of the eldest son (the marriage that, by Er's wickedness and death, v.7, would leave Tamar a childless widow and set the levirate history in motion, vv.8-11); 'whose name was Tamar' (the woman whose persistence, and stratagem, vv.14-26, would secure her the seed denied her, and who would become an ancestress of David and of Christ, Matthew 1:3, 'Judas begat Phares and Zara of Thamar,' the notable figure introduced) — Gill.

themes: And Judah took a wife for Er his firstborn, whose name was Tamar
Judah taking Tamar as wife for Er
the wife of the eldest son, who would be widowed (v.7)
Tamar, an ancestress of David and of Christ (Matthew 1:3)

schools.summary: Er's wickedness and the LORD slaying him: 'and Er, Judah's firstborn, was wicked in the sight of the LORD; and the LORD slew him' — Er wicked (the particular sin not named, but grievous in God's sight, the firstborn of Judah given to evil, the fruit of the Canaanite mother and the worldly home, the wickedness that brought God's judgment); Er wicked 'in the sight of the LORD' (his sin open before God, whatever its precise form, some conjecturing an unnatural sin, as Onan's, v.9, the divine notice of the wickedness, the judgment of God on the evil firstborn); 'the LORD slew him' (God putting Er to death for his wickedness, a direct judgment, as later Nadab and Abihu, Leviticus 10:2; Ananias, Acts 5:5, the sovereign judgment on sin, Er dying childless, leaving Tamar a widow, the occasion of the levirate, v.8, the warning against wickedness) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Er, Judah's firstborn, was wicked in the sight of the LORD; and the LORD slew him
Er's wickedness and the LORD slaying him
the evil firstborn, his sin open before God
the divine judgment on sin (Leviticus 10:2; Acts 5:5), leaving Tamar a widow

schools.summary: Judah charges Onan with the levirate duty: 'and Judah said unto Onan, Go in unto thy brother's wife, and marry her, and raise up seed to thy brother' — Judah charging Onan to perform the levirate duty: to marry Tamar (his dead brother's widow) and raise up seed (children) to the deceased brother's name (the custom of the levirate, the brother marrying the childless widow to continue the dead brother's line and name, later codified in the law, Deuteronomy 25:5-10; Matthew 22:24, the duty owed to the dead); the levirate marriage — the brother (or near kinsman) marrying the widow to raise seed to the dead (the preservation of the family name and inheritance, the ancient custom, preceding the Mosaic law, the duty charged on Onan); 'raise up seed to thy brother' (the firstborn of the levirate union to be reckoned to the dead brother, his name and line continued, Deuteronomy 25:6, the purpose of the duty, the seed that Onan would selfishly refuse, v.9, the custom that figures in Ruth, 4:5-10, and the line of David) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Judah said unto Onan, Go in unto thy brother's wife, and marry her, and raise up seed to thy brother
Judah charging Onan with the levirate duty
the levirate marriage to continue the dead brother's name (Deuteronomy 25:5-6)
the duty owed to the dead, the custom that figures in Ruth (4:5-10)

schools.summary: Onan's refusal of the levirate seed: 'and Onan knew that the seed should not be his; and it came to pass, when he went in unto his brother's wife, that he spilled it on the ground, lest that he should give seed to his brother' — Onan knowing that the child of the levirate union would be reckoned to his dead brother (Er), not to himself (Deuteronomy 25:6, the firstborn would bear the dead brother's name and inheritance, not Onan's, the selfish calculation, the motive of his sin); Onan, though going in to Tamar (taking the pleasure of the marriage), 'spilled it on the ground' — frustrating the purpose of the levirate, that he might not raise up seed to his brother (the sin bearing a double aspect: (a) the malicious refusal of the levirate duty — Onan's covetous, envious unwillingness to raise up seed to his dead brother, grudging the inheritance, perhaps coveting it himself, the breach of the duty owed to the dead and to Tamar, the selfish, fraudulent act, taking the marriage but denying its end; (b) the act itself — the wasting/spilling of the seed, whence the term 'onanism' — an unnatural, abominable deed, the dominant reading holding the chief sin to be the malicious, selfish refusal, the heart that grudged his brother seed, aggravated by the foul means, in either aspect a grievous sin against nature, his brother, and God); Onan's act — taking the pleasure but refusing the duty, and wasting the seed — wicked in God's sight (v.10, the selfishness, fraud, and unnatural deed combined, the sin that displeased the LORD, v.10, and brought his death) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

sides.position: The malicious, selfish refusal of the levirate duty - grudging his dead brother seed and inheritance (the chief sin, the dominant reading), the breach of the duty owed to the dead and to Tamar
The unnatural act itself - the wasting/spilling of the seed (whence 'onanism'), an abominable deed against nature; aggravating the refusal

themes: And Onan knew that the seed should not be his... that he spilled it on the ground, lest that he should give seed to his brother
Onan's refusal of the levirate seed (the crux of his sin)
the malicious, selfish refusal of the levirate duty (grudging his brother seed, the chief sin)
aggravated by the unnatural act itself (whence 'onanism'), a grievous sin against nature and God

schools.summary: The LORD slaying Onan for his sin: 'and the thing which he did displeased the LORD: wherefore he slew him also' — Onan's act (v.9) evil in God's sight (the selfish refusal of the levirate duty, and the unnatural deed, the sin that displeased, was evil in the eyes of, the LORD, the divine judgment on the wickedness, the act condemned by God); the deed 'displeased the LORD' (evil in his sight, the fraud against his brother and Tamar, the wasting of the seed, the contempt of the duty, the sin that God judged, the wickedness condemned); 'wherefore he slew him also' (God putting Onan to death, as Er, v.7, the direct judgment on his sin, both wicked sons of Judah slain by God, the warning against the like selfishness and uncleanness, the two deaths leaving Tamar twice widowed, and Judah fearing for Shelah, v.11) — Gill, JFB, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And the thing which he did displeased the LORD: wherefore he slew him also
the LORD slaying Onan for his sin
the selfish, unnatural deed condemned and judged
both wicked sons of Judah slain, Tamar twice widowed

schools.summary: Judah sends Tamar home, withholding Shelah: 'then said Judah to Tamar his daughter in law, Remain a widow at thy father's house, till Shelah my son be grown: for he said, Lest peradventure he die also, as his brethren did. And she went and dwelt in her father's house' — Judah sending Tamar to her father's house as a widow, with the promise that she should have Shelah (his third son) when he was grown (the levirate duty deferred to Shelah, the seeming promise, the widow sent home to wait); the secret motive — Judah feared 'lest peradventure he die also, as his brethren did' (Er and Onan, vv.7, 10, Judah superstitiously blaming Tamar, as if she were the cause of his sons' deaths, not their own wickedness, the unjust suspicion, the real intent to withhold Shelah, not to give him at all, as v.14, 26 show, the deceitful deferral); 'she went and dwelt in her father's house' (Tamar obeying, returning to her father's house as a widow, awaiting Shelah, the long wait, Shelah grown, yet not given, v.14, the wrong done to Tamar, denied her levirate right, the occasion of her stratagem, vv.13-19) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Then said Judah to Tamar his daughter in law, Remain a widow at thy father's house, till Shelah my son be grown... And she went and dwelt in her father's house
Judah sending Tamar home, withholding Shelah
the levirate deferred (in truth withheld, v.14), with the unjust fear blaming Tamar
the wrong to Tamar that occasioned her stratagem (vv.13-19)

schools.summary: The death of Judah's wife and his going to the sheepshearing: 'and in process of time the daughter of Shuah Judah's wife died; and Judah was comforted, and went up unto his sheepshearers to Timnath, he and his friend Hirah the Adullamite' — Judah's Canaanite wife (v.2) dying, and after the time of mourning Judah being comforted (his grief past, the setting for the events with Tamar, Judah now a widower, the passage of time, Shelah now grown, v.14); Judah, comforted, going up to Timnath to his sheepshearers (the sheepshearing a time of feasting and festivity, as 1 Samuel 25:4-8; 2 Samuel 13:23, the occasion for the journey, the festive time, the setting for Tamar's stratagem, vv.13-19); 'he and his friend Hirah the Adullamite' (Judah with his Canaanite friend, v.1, the worldly company, the journey to the sheepshearing, the occasion Tamar would use, learning of it, v.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Ellicott.

themes: And in process of time the daughter of Shuah Judah's wife died; and Judah was comforted, and went up unto his sheepshearers to Timnath, he and his friend Hirah the Adullamite
the death of Judah's wife and his going to the sheepshearing
the widower going to the festive sheepshearing (1 Samuel 25:4)
the occasion Tamar would use (v.13)

schools.summary: Tamar learns of Judah's journey: 'and it was told Tamar, saying, Behold thy father in law goeth up to Timnath to shear his sheep' — Tamar told that Judah (her father-in-law) was going to Timnath to the sheepshearing (the news that gave her the opportunity, the occasion of her stratagem, vv.14-19, the report); Tamar learning of Judah's journey — the occasion to carry out her plan (to get by Judah the seed denied her, Shelah being withheld, v.11, the report that set her stratagem in motion); 'to shear his sheep' (the sheepshearing, a festive time, v.12, the journey along the road Tamar would watch, v.14, the opportunity for the disguise, the setting for the meeting, vv.14-18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And it was told Tamar, saying, Behold thy father in law goeth up to Timnath to shear his sheep
Tamar learning of Judah's journey
the news that gave her the opportunity for her stratagem
the seed denied her by the withholding of Shelah (v.11)

schools.summary: Tamar's stratagem and her motive: 'and she put her widow's garments off from her, and covered her with a vail, and wrapped herself, and sat in an open place, which is by the way to Timnath; for she saw that Shelah was grown, and she was not given unto him to wife' — Tamar laying aside her widow's clothes, veiling herself (as a harlot, v.15), and sitting by the road (the disguise to deceive Judah, the stratagem to obtain by him the seed denied her, the veiled figure by the way); the reason given — 'for she saw that Shelah was grown, and she was not given unto him to wife': Tamar acting because she had been wronged — Shelah, grown, had not been given her (Judah withholding the levirate right, v.11, her motive to obtain the seed/offspring that was her due by the levirate, to raise up seed to her dead husband, and to have a part in the family of Judah, with its promise, the witnesses careful: Tamar's end, the levirate seed, her right, distinguished from her means, the deceit and the act of harlotry, the means not justified — the deceit and the unchaste act faulty, yet her motive not mere lust but the desire for the promised seed, some of the fathers even commending her zeal for the seed of Judah, the line of promise, the dominant reading: Tamar sinning in the means, yet 'more righteous' than Judah, v.26, who had wronged her by withholding Shelah and then condemned her hypocritically, v.24); Tamar's act, faulty in its means, provoked by Judah's wrong (the withholding of Shelah, v.11, her zeal for the seed, the levirate right, the motive, the providence overruling even the sin to bring forth Pharez, the line of the Messiah, vv.29; Matthew 1:3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: How is Tamar's stratagem to be judged?

sides.position: Her motive (the levirate seed, her right, denied by Judah's withholding of Shelah, v.11) distinguished from her means (the deceit and harlotry, not justified) - Tamar sinning in the means, yet 'more righteous' than Judah (v.26)
Her zeal for the seed of promise (the line of Judah) the motive, even commended by some - overruled by God to bring forth Pharez (Matthew 1:3)

themes: And she put her widow's garments off from her, and covered her with a vail... for she saw that Shelah was grown, and she was not given unto him to wife
Tamar's stratagem and her motive (the crux of her act)
the motive (the levirate seed, her right) distinguished from the means (deceit and harlotry, not justified)
Tamar sinning in the means, yet 'more righteous' than Judah (v.26), overruled to bring forth Pharez (Matthew 1:3)

schools.summary: Judah mistakes Tamar for a harlot: 'when Judah saw her, he thought her to be an harlot; because she had covered her face' — Judah, seeing the veiled woman by the road, taking her for a harlot (a prostitute, the veil being, in that culture, a mark by which the harlot concealed herself, the mistake, Judah not recognizing his daughter-in-law, the veil hiding her, the occasion of his sin); the covered face marking her (to Judah) as a harlot (or, by v.21, a 'kedeshah,' a consecrated prostitute of the Canaanite cult, the veil the disguise, Judah's mistaking her, the sad occasion); 'because she had covered her face' (the veil hiding Tamar's identity, Judah did not know her, the disguise that deceived, the cover of the face, Judah's sin, going in to a supposed harlot — the fault that Tamar's stratagem exposed, and that his confession, v.26, would own, the descent of Judah, the contrast with Joseph, ch. 39) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: When Judah saw her, he thought her to be an harlot; because she had covered her face
Judah mistaking Tamar for a harlot
the veil hiding Tamar's identity, the occasion of Judah's sin
the descent of Judah, set against Joseph's purity (ch. 39)

schools.summary: Judah's solicitation and Tamar's demand of a pledge: 'and he turned unto her by the way, and said, Go to, I pray thee, let me come in unto thee; (for he knew not that she was his daughter in law.) And she said, What wilt thou give me, that thou mayest come in unto me?' — Judah soliciting the supposed harlot (the sin of the patriarch, going in to a prostitute, not knowing her his daughter-in-law, the descent of Judah, the parenthesis 'for he knew not that she was his daughter in law' marking his ignorance, the disguise, v.15); Judah not knowing her (the veil deceived him, his sin, intended fornication, real, though the partner was his daughter-in-law, the ignorance that the disguise produced, the fault of Judah); Tamar (acting the harlot's part) demanding a price (the bargain that led to the pledge, vv.17-18, her stratagem to obtain the tokens, the signet, bracelets, staff, that would prove the father, v.25, the demand that set the trap) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB, Calvin.

themes: And he turned unto her by the way, and said, Go to, I pray thee, let me come in unto thee... What wilt thou give me, that thou mayest come in unto me?
Judah's solicitation and Tamar's demand of a pledge
Judah's sin, not knowing her his daughter-in-law (the disguise, v.15)
Tamar's stratagem to obtain the tokens that would prove the father (v.25)

schools.summary: Judah's offer of a kid and Tamar's demand of a pledge: 'and he said, I will send thee a kid from the flock. And she said, Wilt thou give me a pledge, till thou send it?' — Judah offering a kid (a young goat) as the price, Tamar asking a pledge (a security) until the kid was sent (the bargain, Tamar's stratagem to obtain the tokens, vv.18, 25, the demand of a pledge); the kid offered — the price for the supposed harlot (the payment to be sent, the bargain struck, yet the kid would not reach her, the messenger not finding her, vv.20-23); 'Wilt thou give me a pledge, till thou send it?' (Tamar asking a security until the kid came, the pledge, the signet, bracelets, staff, v.18, that would later prove Judah the father, v.25, the stratagem's key, the demand that secured the tokens) — Gill, Clarke, Wesley.

themes: And he said, I will send thee a kid from the flock. And she said, Wilt thou give me a pledge, till thou send it?
Judah's offer of a kid and Tamar's demand of a pledge
the security (the tokens that would prove the father, v.25)
the key to Tamar's stratagem

schools.summary: The pledge given and Tamar conceiving: 'and he said, What pledge shall I give thee? And she said, Thy signet, and thy bracelets, and thy staff that is in thine hand. And he gave it her, and came in unto her, and she conceived by him' — Tamar asking for Judah's signet (his seal-ring, the mark of his identity), the cord/bracelets (the band by which the seal hung), and his staff (with its distinctive carving, the tokens of identity, easily recognized, the pledge given, the proof Tamar secured, v.25); these personal tokens (the signet especially, the seal of identity) — chosen by Tamar precisely because they would prove Judah the father (the shrewd securing of the evidence, the pledge that would condemn Judah's hypocrisy, v.25); 'he gave it her, and came in unto her, and she conceived by him' (Judah giving the pledge and going in, Tamar conceiving, the seed she sought, the levirate right denied her, v.11, the conception of the twins, Pharez and Zarah, vv.27-30, the providence, overruling the sin, bringing forth the line of the Messiah, Pharez, Matthew 1:3) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And he said, What pledge shall I give thee? And she said, Thy signet, and thy bracelets, and thy staff that is in thine hand. And he gave it her, and came in unto her, and she conceived by him
the pledge given and Tamar conceiving
the tokens of identity (the signet, the proof secured, v.25)
the conception overruled by God to bring forth Pharez (Matthew 1:3)

schools.summary: Tamar resumes her widow's garments: 'and she arose, and went away, and laid by her vail from her, and put on the garments of her widowhood' — Tamar, her purpose accomplished (the conception, v.18), removing the veil (the harlot's disguise) and putting on her widow's clothes again (the resuming of her widowhood, the disguise laid aside, the return to her father's house, v.11, as a widow); Tamar laying aside the veil (the disguise, v.14) — the stratagem done (the harlot's appearance gone, the return to her widow's state); 'put on the garments of her widowhood' (Tamar resuming her widow's clothes, v.11, the appearance of the chaste widow restored, the concealment of her act, until the proof, v.25, the waiting for the issue, the conception that would be discovered, v.24) — Gill.

themes: And she arose, and went away, and laid by her vail from her, and put on the garments of her widowhood
Tamar resuming her widow's garments
her purpose accomplished (v.18), the disguise removed (v.14)
the chaste widow's appearance restored, her act concealed (until v.25)

schools.summary: Judah sends the kid to redeem his pledge: 'and Judah sent the kid by the hand of his friend the Adullamite, to receive his pledge from the woman's hand: but he found her not' — Judah sending the promised kid (v.17) by Hirah his Adullamite friend (v.1), to pay the supposed harlot and recover his pledge (the signet, bracelets, staff, v.18, the honoring of the bargain, the attempt to redeem the tokens); Hirah (Judah's Canaanite friend, v.1) sent — the go-between for the transaction (the discreet errand, to keep the matter private, the friend in the worldly business); 'but he found her not' (Hirah could not find the woman, Tamar having resumed her widowhood, v.19, and gone home, the pledge not recovered, the providence keeping the tokens in Tamar's hand, the proof, v.25, the kid not delivered) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Judah sent the kid by the hand of his friend the Adullamite, to receive his pledge from the woman's hand: but he found her not
Judah sending the kid to redeem his pledge
the attempt to recover the tokens (vv.17-18) by Hirah his friend
the providence keeping the proof in Tamar's hand (v.25)

schools.summary: The search for the supposed harlot: 'then he asked the men of that place, saying, Where is the harlot, that was openly by the way side? And they said, There was no harlot in this place' — Hirah asking the men of the place for the 'harlot' (Hebrew kedeshah, consecrated one, a temple-prostitute of the Canaanite cult, a more respectable term than the common harlot, the men knowing of none, the search fruitless, the providence concealing Tamar); 'kedeshah' (consecrated prostitute) — the term used (perhaps to soften the matter, or as the Canaanite custom of cult-prostitution, the search for her, the men's reply that there was none); 'There was no harlot in this place' (the men knowing of no such woman, Tamar gone, v.19, the search ended, the tokens not recovered, the providence keeping the proof, v.25) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: Then he asked the men of that place, saying, Where is the harlot, that was openly by the way side? And they said, There was no harlot in this place
the search for the supposed harlot (the kedeshah)
the fruitless inquiry, the men knowing of none (Tamar gone, v.19)
the providence keeping the tokens (the proof, v.25)

schools.summary: Hirah returns, unable to find her: 'and he returned to Judah, and said, I cannot find her; and also the men of the place said, that there was no harlot in this place' — Hirah returning to Judah and reporting that he could not find the woman, and that the men of the place knew of no harlot there (the failed errand, v.20, the pledge not recovered, the report that ended the search); Hirah could not find Tamar (gone, v.19, the unsuccessful errand, the tokens left in her hand); 'there was no harlot in this place' (the men knowing of none, v.21, the search ended, Judah to let the matter rest, v.23, leaving the pledge with Tamar, the proof, v.25, the providence keeping the tokens) — Gill, JFB.

themes: And he returned to Judah, and said, I cannot find her; and also the men of the place said, that there was no harlot in this place
Hirah returning, unable to find her
the failed errand (v.20), the tokens left with Tamar
the providence keeping the proof (v.25)

schools.summary: Judah lets the matter rest, lest he be shamed: 'and Judah said, Let her take it to her, lest we be shamed: behold, I sent this kid, and thou hast not found her' — Judah letting the woman keep the pledge (the signet, bracelets, staff, v.18), rather than search further and be exposed (shamed) by the inquiry after a harlot (the fear of public shame, the prudence, and the irony — the pledge would shame him far more, v.25, the matter dropped); Judah's fear of 'shame' — that further searching for the harlot would expose his conduct (the wish to avoid the embarrassment, the irony that the tokens he abandoned would, in Tamar's hand, bring the very shame he feared, v.25, the dropping of the matter); 'I sent this kid, and thou hast not found her' (Judah, having tried to pay, and failed, v.20, letting it go, the pledge left with Tamar, the proof, v.25, the providence keeping the tokens in her hand, the matter rested, till the discovery, v.24) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And Judah said, Let her take it to her, lest we be shamed: behold, I sent this kid, and thou hast not found her
Judah letting the matter rest, lest he be shamed
the fear of public exposure, the matter dropped
the irony - the tokens he abandoned would shame him far more (v.25)

schools.summary: Judah's hasty, hypocritical sentence on Tamar: 'and it came to pass about three months after, that it was told Judah, saying, Tamar thy daughter in law hath played the harlot; and also, behold, she is with child by whoredom. And Judah said, Bring her forth, and let her be burnt' — Tamar's pregnancy becoming evident, she reported to Judah as having played the harlot (the accusation against the betrothed widow, Tamar, as betrothed to Shelah, v.11, reckoned bound to the family, so her pregnancy deemed adultery/whoredom, the charge brought); Judah saying 'Bring her forth, and let her be burnt' — Judah, as head of the family (and judge), passing a hasty, severe sentence: that Tamar be burnt (the penalty for the harlotry/adultery of one betrothed, perhaps to the family, or as a priest's daughter, Leviticus 21:9, the severe punishment, the witnesses marking the glaring hypocrisy — Judah, who had himself just gone in to a harlot, vv.16-18, now hotly condemning Tamar for the very sin, the unjust haste, the double standard, the irony that he condemned in her what he had done, and worse, having wronged her, v.11, the self-condemnation about to be exposed, v.25); Judah's quick, fierce judgment of Tamar (while excusing his own sin) displaying the hypocrisy of the man who judges in another what he does himself (Romans 2:1; Matthew 7:3-5, the providence about to turn the sentence back on him, v.25, the warning against the censorious, hypocritical heart) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

debates.question: What does Judah's sentence 'let her be burnt' display?

sides.position: The glaring hypocrisy of Judah - who had just gone in to a supposed harlot (vv.16-18), now fiercely condemning Tamar for the very sin; the man judging in another what he did himself (Romans 2:1; Matthew 7:3-5), the self-condemnation about to be exposed (v.25)

themes: And it came to pass about three months after... Tamar thy daughter in law hath played the harlot... And Judah said, Bring her forth, and let her be burnt
Judah's hasty, hypocritical sentence on Tamar (the crux of hypocrisy)
Judah, who had just gone in to a harlot (vv.16-18), condemning Tamar for the very sin
the man judging in another what he did himself (Romans 2:1; Matthew 7:3-5)

schools.summary: Tamar produces the tokens to identify the father: 'when she was brought forth, she sent to her father in law, saying, By the man, whose these are, am I with child: and she said, Discern, I pray thee, whose are these, the signet, and bracelets, and staff' — Tamar, brought out for execution (v.24), sending privately to Judah the tokens (the pledge, v.18), declaring that the owner of them was the father of her child (the proof produced, the quiet, dignified vindication, not openly exposing Judah, but privately convicting him, the evidence presented); Tamar asking Judah to 'discern' (recognize) whose the signet, bracelets, and staff were — the tokens of identity (v.18, the appeal to his own conscience, the proof that left no doubt, the modest manner, sparing him public exposure, yet convicting him); 'the signet, and bracelets, and staff' (the very pledge Judah had given, v.18, the irrefutable proof that he was the father, the tokens turning his sentence back on himself, v.24, the providence, and Tamar's prudence, bringing the truth to light, the conviction of Judah, his confession, v.26) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: When she was brought forth, she sent to her father in law, saying, By the man, whose these are, am I with child... Discern, I pray thee, whose are these, the signet, and bracelets, and staff
Tamar producing the tokens to identify the father
the quiet, dignified vindication, privately convicting Judah by his own pledge (v.18)
the tokens turning his sentence back on himself (v.24), sparing him public exposure

schools.summary: Judah's confession that Tamar was more righteous: 'and Judah acknowledged them, and said, She hath been more righteous than I; because that I gave her not to Shelah my son. And he knew her again no more' — Judah recognizing the tokens (the signet, bracelets, staff, v.25) as his own (the proof undeniable, the conviction, the patriarch owning the truth, rather than denying or destroying the evidence, the honest acknowledgment); Judah's words — 'She hath been more righteous than I; because that I gave her not to Shelah my son': Judah confessing that Tamar was 'more righteous' than he — not that her act (the deceit and harlotry) was righteous (the witnesses careful: her means were faulty), but that she was less to blame than he, who had wronged her by withholding Shelah (v.11) and so driving her to the stratagem (Judah owning his greater fault, the breach of the levirate duty, and his own sin with the supposed harlot, the honest, humbling confession of the patriarch, a mark of grace, that he confessed rather than excused, before all, the self-condemnation, his own sentence, v.24, turned on himself, some taking 'more righteous' as her cause being just, the levirate seed her right, while his withholding was unjust); 'he knew her again no more' (Judah not taking Tamar as a wife, the levirate now fulfilled in the conception, and the relation being incestuous, daughter-in-law, the matter ended, Tamar's vindication, the twins born, Pharez, Zarah, vv.27-30, the line of the Messiah, Matthew 1:3, through Tamar and Judah) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

debates.question: What does Judah's 'She hath been more righteous than I' mean?

sides.position: Not that Tamar's deceit was righteous (her means faulty), but that she was less to blame than Judah, who wronged her by withholding Shelah (v.11) and condemned her hypocritically (v.24); the honest, humbling self-condemnation of the patriarch

themes: And Judah acknowledged them, and said, She hath been more righteous than I; because that I gave her not to Shelah my son. And he knew her again no more
Judah's confession that Tamar was more righteous (the crux of the confession)
not that her deceit was righteous, but that she was less to blame than Judah, who withheld Shelah (v.11)
the honest, humbling self-condemnation of the patriarch (a mark of grace)

schools.summary: Twins in Tamar's womb: 'and it came to pass in the time of her travail, that, behold, twins were in her womb' — Tamar, at her delivery, found to bear twins (the two sons, Pharez and Zarah, vv.29-30, the providence multiplying the seed, the levirate seed, doubled, the twins who would head two families of Judah, and Pharez the line of the Messiah, Matthew 1:3); the twins — the double blessing on the seed (the providence giving Tamar, and Judah, two sons, the parallel with Rebekah's twins, 25:24, the two who would contend at the birth, vv.28-30); the twins (Pharez and Zarah) would head families of Judah (Pharez the ancestor of David and of Christ, Ruth 4:18-22; Matthew 1:3, the providence bringing forth, through the scandal, the royal and Messianic line, the seed of promise continued) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass in the time of her travail, that, behold, twins were in her womb
twins in Tamar's womb
the providence multiplying the seed (the two sons, as Rebekah's twins, 25:24)
the line of the Messiah through Pharez (Matthew 1:3), brought forth through the scandal

schools.summary: The scarlet thread on the hand of the first twin: 'and it came to pass, when she travailed, that the one put out his hand: and the midwife took and bound upon his hand a scarlet thread, saying, This came out first' — at the birth, one twin putting out his hand first, the midwife tying a scarlet thread on it to mark him as the first (the firstborn, by appearance, the contest of the twins at the birth, as Jacob and Esau, 25:25-26, the marking of the supposed firstborn, Zarah, v.30); the scarlet thread — to mark the hand that came first (Zarah, 'brightness/scarlet,' the token of the firstborn's place, the mark that the breach, v.29, would overturn, Pharez, the second hand, coming out first); 'This came out first' (the midwife's word, marking the hand as the firstborn's, yet the other, Pharez, would break forth first, v.29, overturning the order, as Jacob supplanted Esau, 25:26, the strange birth, the providence in the order, Pharez, not Zarah, the line of Christ, Matthew 1:3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass, when she travailed, that the one put out his hand: and the midwife took and bound upon his hand a scarlet thread, saying, This came out first
the scarlet thread on the hand of the first twin
the marking of the supposed firstborn (Zarah), the contest at the birth (as 25:25-26)
the order the breach of Pharez would overturn (v.29)

schools.summary: The breach of Pharez: 'and it came to pass, as he drew back his hand, that, behold, his brother came out: and she said, How hast thou broken forth? this breach be upon thee: therefore his name was called Pharez' — the first twin drawing back his hand (marked by the scarlet thread, v.28), and the other (Pharez) breaking forth and being born first (the unexpected reversal, the second becoming the first, as Jacob over Esau, 25:26, the younger preferred, the strange birth); the midwife (or Tamar) saying, 'How hast thou broken forth? this breach be upon thee' (Pharez, 'breach,' 'a breaking forth,' named for the breach he made, breaking out before his brother, the name marking the violent, unexpected birth, the breach by which he gained precedence, the second made first); Pharez, who broke forth and gained the firstborn's place, becoming the ancestor of the royal and Messianic line — the line of David (Ruth 4:18-22, 'Pharez begat Hezron... Jesse, and Jesse begat David') and of Christ ('Judas begat Phares and Zara of Thamar...,' Matthew 1:3; Luke 3:33, the providence bringing forth, through the scandal of Judah and Tamar, the line of the Messiah, the grace that wrought the line of promise through unlikely, even sinful, means, Pharez the progenitor of the Christ, the breach-maker the head of the chosen line) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What is the significance of Pharez's breach?

sides.position: Pharez ('breach'), the second who broke forth to be first (as Jacob over Esau, 25:26), became the ancestor of David and of Christ (Ruth 4:18-22; Matthew 1:3; Luke 3:33) - the providence bringing forth the line of the Messiah through the scandal of Judah and Tamar, grace through unlikely means

themes: And it came to pass, as he drew back his hand, that, behold, his brother came out... therefore his name was called Pharez
the breach of Pharez (the crux of the Messianic line)
Pharez ('breach'), the second made first (as 25:26), ancestor of David and of Christ (Ruth 4:18-22; Matthew 1:3)
the providence bringing forth the line of the Messiah through the scandal, grace through unlikely means

schools.summary: The birth of Zarah: 'and afterward came out his brother, that had the scarlet thread upon his hand: and his name was called Zarah' — the second to be born (though his hand came first, v.28) Zarah ('rising,' 'brightness,' or 'scarlet,' from the thread, the twin marked by the scarlet thread, the brother of Pharez, the head of a family of Judah, the Zarhites, Numbers 26:20); Zarah bearing the scarlet thread (v.28) — the mark of the hand that first appeared (yet born second, after Pharez's breach, v.29, the supposed firstborn become the second); 'his name was called Zarah' (the second twin named Zarah, the brother of Pharez, the head of the Zarhites, the twins, Pharez and Zarah, completing the birth, the line of promise, through Pharez, continued, Matthew 1:3, the close of the Judah-and-Tamar history) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And afterward came out his brother, that had the scarlet thread upon his hand: and his name was called Zarah
the birth of Zarah
the supposed firstborn (the scarlet thread, v.28) become the second, the head of the Zarhites
the line of promise continued through Pharez (Matthew 1:3)
